_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 4-9 Pediatric Crashing Patient/Impending Arrest (Revised 05/25/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan Obstetrics and Pediatrics Protocol Section 4-9: Pediatric Crashing Patient/Impending Arrest — MDHHS Approved 5/25/23, Reviewed 2023
purpose: Rapidly recognize pediatric patients who are critically ill and deteriorating toward cardiac or respiratory arrest and initiate goal-directed treatments before loading and transport.

sections.title: Criteria
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 4-9: Pediatric Crashing Patient — Criteria
points: Patient is younger than 14 years of age
Inclusion: cardiac or respiratory arrest appears imminent
Inclusion: provider impression of critical illness, including new onset altered mental status, airway compromise, severe respiratory distress/failure, or shock/poor perfusion
Severe respiratory distress/failure includes cyanosis, severe retractions, head bobbing, grunting, and respiratory-rate extremes per MI-MEDIC
Shock/poor perfusion includes capillary refill greater than 3 seconds, tachycardia, or hypotension per age-adjusted normal on MI-MEDIC
Exclusion: life-threatening trauma that has not been corrected, such as exsanguination or pneumothorax

sections.title: Critical Actions — Within First 5 Minutes
points: Open airway manually; for a child younger than 2 years, place padding under the shoulders to align auditory meatus with sternal notch
Insert NPA or OPA as indicated/tolerated if not following commands, GCS motor < 6, GCS < 9, or no response to verbal stimuli per Airway Management-Procedure Protocol
If respiratory failure or distress, sit patient up if tolerated and not contraindicated by suspected spine injury; keep patient calm and allow position of comfort if possible
Provide high-flow oxygen per Oxygen Administration-Procedure Protocol
If respirations are less than 10/min or inadequate, ventilate by BVM at 15 LPM; use chest rise to guide ventilations
Two-person, two-handed BVM technique is most effective and strongly recommended when staffing allows
If respirations are greater than 10/min but inadequate, apply CPAP for respiratory distress/hypoxia if appropriate size CPAP is available
Respirations may be assisted with BVM in sitting position if patient tolerates
Consider PPV by BVM if not following commands or SpO2 is persistently less than 90%
Consider waveform capnography per End Tidal Carbon Dioxide Monitoring-Procedure Protocol
Reference MI-MEDIC cards for age-adjusted expected blood pressure and heart rate ranges
If bradycardic with HR < 60, optimize ventilation and oxygenation and refer to Pediatric Bradycardia-Treatment Protocol
Emergent IV/IO access: limit IV attempts to 2 total; for unresponsive or severely compromised pediatrics, IO can be the initial attempt
Monitor NIBP every 3 minutes, SpO2, continuous capnography when appropriate, and EKG

sections.title: Immediate Actions — Within First 10 Minutes
points: If evidence of poor perfusion, administer NS or LR 20 mL/kg bolus unless cardiogenic shock is suspected
If cardiogenic shock is suspected, such as JVD, hepatomegaly, abdominal distension, or crackles, administer 5–10 mL/kg NS bolus instead and contact Medical Control
If dysrhythmia is thought to be the primary cause of shock, contact Medical Control to discuss further interventions such as cardioversion or pacing

sections.title: Actions — Within First 15 Minutes
points: Reassess response to treatments, including capillary refill with vital signs
Recheck vitals and listen to lungs after fluid bolus
If decreasing oxygen saturations, crackles, or worsening respiratory distress occur, STOP fluid bolus and contact Medical Control immediately
Consider starting vasopressors per Shock-Treatment Protocol
Repeat NS or LR 20 mL/kg bolus if indicated, maximum total dose 40 mL/kg
If bradycardia with HR < 60, optimize ventilation and oxygenation and refer to Pediatric Bradycardia-Treatment Protocol
If no response to fluids, follow Shock-Treatment Protocol

sections.title: Actions — Within First 20 Minutes
points: Reassess response to treatments
Continue fluids as indicated by Shock-Treatment Protocol or contact Medical Control
Continue vasopressors as indicated by Shock-Treatment Protocol or contact Medical Control
Insert advanced airway if indicated and appropriate size is available per Airway Management-Procedure Protocol

sections.title: Transport and Notes
points: Once critical and immediate actions have been completed, move the patient to the ambulance for transport
Transport may be initiated earlier per provider discretion
Time targets are approximate to prioritize urgency; provider safety and scene conditions may make times longer and reasons should be documented
Actions should be simultaneous and not in a strict order
Transport should not supersede initiation of life-saving intervention
These concepts may be used with Pediatric ROSC-Treatment Protocol to prioritize interventions before transport after ROSC

key_drugs.name: NS or LR IV/IO Fluid Bolus (Pediatric)
key_drugs.dose: 20 mL/kg IV/IO bolus; 5–10 mL/kg if suspected cardiogenic shock; maximum total 40 mL/kg
key_drugs.indication: Poor perfusion or shock in pediatric crashing patient
key_drugs.contraindications: Suspected cardiogenic shock requires smaller 5–10 mL/kg bolus and Medical Control contact; stop bolus if pulmonary edema or worsening respiratory distress develops
key_drugs.side_effects: Pulmonary edema, fluid overload, worsening respiratory distress
key_drugs.notes: Recheck vitals and listen to lungs after bolus. Stop fluids and contact Medical Control if oxygen saturation decreases, crackles develop, or respiratory distress worsens.

deterioration_flags: SpO2 persistently less than 90% or not following commands — consider PPV by BVM with 15 LPM oxygen
Respirations less than 10/min or inadequate — ventilate by BVM at 15 LPM using chest rise
HR less than 60 — optimize ventilation/oxygenation and refer to Pediatric Bradycardia protocol
Decreasing oxygen saturation, crackles, or worsening respiratory distress during fluids — stop bolus and contact Medical Control immediately
No response to fluids — follow Shock protocol
Pulseless patient — initiate CPR and refer to Pediatric Cardiac Arrest protocol
scope_notes: Airway positioning, BVM ventilation, basic airway adjuncts, SpO2 monitoring, and high-flow oxygen: all levels
CPAP: per CPAP-Procedure Protocol if appropriate size is available
EKG/cardiac monitoring, ETCO2/capnography, IV/IO access, and fluid bolus: Specialist skill
Vasopressors and dysrhythmia interventions: as indicated by Shock, Pediatric Bradycardia, or Medical Control guidance
Advanced airway: per Airway Management-Procedure Protocol when indicated and appropriate size available
